articles.title: Psilocybin improves sleep quality in patients with chronic cluster headaches
articles.summary: Psilocybin could help patients with chronic cluster headaches sleep better, according to a recent study in the Journal of Psychopharmacology. Researchers also noted subtle shifts in brain microstructure that tend to accompany these improvements in rest.
articles.aiSummary: Psilocybin could help patients with chronic cluster headaches sleep better, according to a recent study in the Journal of Psychopharmacology. Researchers also noted subtle shifts in brain microstructure that tend to accompany these improvements in rest.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Psilocybin could help patients with chronic cluster headaches sleep better, according to a recent study in the Journal of Psychopharmacology. Researchers also noted subtle shifts in brain microstructure that tend to accompany these improvements in rest.

articles.title: What millions of voter records reveal about political independents
articles.summary: A new analysis challenges the notion that political independents are merely undercover partisans or apathetic citizens. Researchers found that most unaligned voters overwhelmingly prefer moderate ideologies and highly value political flexibility over rigid party labels.
articles.aiSummary: A new analysis challenges the notion that political independents are merely undercover partisans or apathetic citizens. Researchers found that most unaligned voters overwhelmingly prefer moderate ideologies and highly value political flexibility over rigid party labels.

articles.title: Major new study links childhood income inequality to a magnified genetic risk for depression
articles.summary: A large international twin study reveals that childhood economic inequality shapes adult mental health. Published in Psychological Medicine, the research provides evidence that a wide wealth gap can magnify a person's genetic risk for developing depression.
articles.aiSummary: A large international twin study reveals that childhood economic inequality shapes adult mental health. Published in Psychological Medicine, the research provides evidence that a wide wealth gap can magnify a person's genetic risk for developing depression.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A large international twin study reveals that childhood economic inequality shapes adult mental health. Published in Psychological Medicine, the research provides evidence that a wide wealth gap can magnify a person's genetic risk for developing depression.

articles.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.00397v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Understanding how complex cognitive functions are organized within artificial systems is central to interpreting large language models (LLMs) and relating them to biological cognition. Yet although LLMs exhibit broad cognitive-like behaviours, it remains unclear whether their internal representations form reproducible functional systems that explain behaviour, failure and links to human cognition. Here we present NeuroCogMap, a cognitive neuroscience-inspired framework that organizes internal features of LLMs into functional parcels and links the

articles.title: DRIADA: A Python Toolkit for Cross-Scale Analysis of Single-Neuron Selectivity and Population Dynamics
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.00851v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Brain activity spans single-neuron, population, and network levels, and core questions in neural coding require moving between them. Yet current tools target a single paradigm and incompatible data formats, leaving cross-level questions hard to address. We present DRIADA, an open-source Python framework that unifies neural signals and time-aligned behavior in a shared data model, so selectivity testing, dimensionality reduction, and network analysis operate within a unified workflow. We evaluate it on synthetic data with known ground truth, hippo

articles.title: Reward function compression facilitates goal-dependent reinforcement learning
articles.summary: arXiv:2509.06810v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: Humans can uniquely assign value to novel, abstract outcomes to support reinforcement learning. However, this flexibility is cognitively costly and reduces learning efficiency. We propose that goal-dependent learning initially relies on capacity-limited working memory. With consistent experience, learners create a "compressed" reward function - a simplified goal rule -- that transfers to long-term memory for a more automatic evaluation upon receiving feedback. This automaticity frees working memory resources, thereby boosting learning efficie

articles.title: Neuronal electricality founded in murburn-thermodynamic principles: 1. Background and basic theoretical formulation
articles.summary: arXiv:2604.24772v2 Announce Type: replace Abstract: Trans-membrane ion-gradients and fluxes are central to conventional electrical activity in aerobic cells/organelles. The Murburn concept offers novel physico-chemical models for various metabolic, bioenergetic and electrophysiological phenomena. Here, we develop a foundational framework for neuronal electrical activity and axonal signal propagation using the electron-holding potential (EHP), a dimensionless field related logarithmically to electron chemical potential. articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. What is the "dignity of risk"? And how do you know which "version" of a person to trust? [buzzsprout episode='19262736' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: Both men and women rate female faces as more attractive
articles.summary: A massive new data review combining 1.5 million ratings from across the globe confirms a long-debated phenomenon: people consistently rate female faces as more attractive than male faces.
articles.aiSummary: A massive new data review combining 1.5 million ratings from across the globe confirms a long-debated phenomenon: people consistently rate female faces as more attractive than male faces.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A massive new data review combining 1.5 million ratings from across the globe confirms a long-debated phenomenon: people consistently rate female faces as more attractive than male faces.

articles.title: Narcissism and psychopathy linked to lower physical stress responses under pressure
articles.summary: People with higher levels of narcissism and psychopathy tend to experience lower physical and psychological reactions to acute stress. A recent study suggests these socially aversive personality traits might actually offer biological resilience during high-pressure situations.
articles.aiSummary: People with higher levels of narcissism and psychopathy tend to experience lower physical and psychological reactions to acute stress. A recent study suggests these socially aversive personality traits might actually offer biological resilience during high-pressure situations.
articles.aiSummaryEn: People with higher levels of narcissism and psychopathy tend to experience lower physical and psychological reactions to acute stress. A recent study suggests these socially aversive personality traits might actually offer biological resilience during high-pressure situations.

articles.title: Video games might offer a small boost to memory and mental skills
articles.summary: A recent overarching review of dozens of studies found that playing video games might offer a modest boost to various mental skills, helping players process information and remember details more effectively.
articles.aiSummary: A recent overarching review of dozens of studies found that playing video games might offer a modest boost to various mental skills, helping players process information and remember details more effectively.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A recent overarching review of dozens of studies found that playing video games might offer a modest boost to various mental skills, helping players process information and remember details more effectively.

articles.title: Breastfeeding during the first six months is linked to better sleep at one year
articles.summary: A new study of over 80,000 infants reveals that babies breastfed for their first six months are less likely to experience short sleep durations at age one, challenging the common belief that formula-fed babies sleep longer.
articles.aiSummary: A new study of over 80,000 infants reveals that babies breastfed for their first six months are less likely to experience short sleep durations at age one, challenging the common belief that formula-fed babies sleep longer.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study of over 80,000 infants reveals that babies breastfed for their first six months are less likely to experience short sleep durations at age one, challenging the common belief that formula-fed babies sleep longer.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.aiSummary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

articles.title: Study explores how cultural tightness shapes personality and political beliefs
articles.summary: Living in a region with strict social norms is linked to distinct psychological traits. A new study found that culturally tight societies often show higher levels of right-wing authoritarianism, racial stereotyping, and a greater need for order.
articles.aiSummary: Living in a region with strict social norms is linked to distinct psychological traits. A new study found that culturally tight societies often show higher levels of right-wing authoritarianism, racial stereotyping, and a greater need for order.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Living in a region with strict social norms is linked to distinct psychological traits. A new study found that culturally tight societies often show higher levels of right-wing authoritarianism, racial stereotyping, and a greater need for order.

articles.title: How belief in karma shapes whom we choose to help
articles.summary: Belief in karma can lead people to view social inequality as fair, but it also motivates charitable giving. New research reveals that focusing on karma increases generosity, but mostly toward individuals who suffer through no fault of their own.
articles.aiSummary: Belief in karma can lead people to view social inequality as fair, but it also motivates charitable giving. New research reveals that focusing on karma increases generosity, but mostly toward individuals who suffer through no fault of their own.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
articles.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.
